---
title: "Continuous Grip-Force Dynamics as an Online Behavioral Signal of Perceptual Strain"
author:
  - name: Mohammad Dastgheib
    corresponding: true
    email: mdastgheib@ucr.edu
    affiliations:
      - name: Department of Psychology, University of California, Riverside
        city: Riverside
        state: CA
        country: USA
date: "2026-06-24"
bibliography: references.bib
abstract: |
  **Background:** Older adults frequently perform perceptual decisions while sustaining physical effort, yet most dual-task research treats motor output as a binary manipulation rather than a continuous behavioral signal. Here we ask whether sub-second grip-force dynamics carry incremental information about trial-level perceptual accuracy beyond nominal task difficulty.
  
  **Methods:** Older adults (*N* ≈ 64, mean age 69.5 years) performed auditory and visual same/different discrimination tasks while maintaining low (5%) or high (40%) isometric handgrip force scaled to maximum voluntary contraction. Event-locked spectral power was extracted from the continuous dynamometer stream in a visuomotor tracking band (0.5–3.0 Hz) and a physiological tremor band (8.0–12.0 Hz). Mixed-effects logistic regression tested whether band-power features improved prediction of perceptual accuracy, and subject-wise cross-validation assessed out-of-sample generalizability.
  
  **Results:** Adding tracking- and tremor-band features did not significantly improve model fit over the baseline psychometric model in the high-grip arm (LRT *p* = .447). Tremor-band power showed a numerically negative association with accuracy (OR ≈ 0.96, *p* = .282) that was similar in direction and magnitude across both grip strata; a formal tremor × grip interaction was not significant, nor was a tremor × difficulty interaction. Cross-validated models confirmed only minimal incremental gain from force features beyond task labels (ΔAUC ≈ 0.005). Exploratory locus coeruleus microstructure analyses showed non-significant directional trends only.
  
  **Conclusion:** Continuous grip-force spectral features provided weak, non-significant inferential evidence for trial-level cognitive-motor coupling in this sample. The results establish a reproducible force-signal pipeline and empirical benchmarks for future confirmatory work on the spectral-perceptual coupling question in aging.
keywords:
  - aging
  - handgrip
  - cognitive-motor interference
  - perceptual decision-making
  - physiological tremor
  - force dynamics
  - locus coeruleus
  - dual-task
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{lineno}
        \linenumbers
---

{{< include _manuscript-content.qmd >}}
